questions.prompt: You're building a phone-based voice assistant. Users speak; the agent talks back. A teammate suggests using a reasoning model so the agent is 'smarter.' How should you respond?

choices.text: Great idea — reasoning models are always better.

Push back. Reasoning models add several seconds of latency per turn because they generate reasoning tokens before output. Voice agents live or die on sub-second response. Use a fast model in the hot path; if you need reasoning, do it offline or behind a 'I'll think about this' acknowledgment.
Use the reasoning model with maximum reasoning_effort to be safe.
Add image generation to the agent for good measure.

questions.explanation: Voice products are dominated by the latency corner of the triangle. Reasoning models trade speed for depth — the opposite of what a voice agent needs. The right pattern: fast model in the hot path, optionally with tool calls to specialized systems. Reasoning belongs in research pipelines, not voice loops.

questions.prompt: A new AI feature is live but slightly buggy and expensive. You can fix one thing first. Which order makes sense?

choices.text: Cut costs first — the bill is what stakeholders see.

Fix quality first. Cost optimization on a broken product is wasted work. Once quality clears the bar via evals, then squeeze cost (caching, compaction, smaller models on easy turns), then optimize latency (streaming, parallel tool calls).

questions.explanation: Order of optimization is quality → cost → latency. Cheap fast garbage is still garbage. Once the product works (evals look good), cost is the next biggest lever (caching and compaction routinely cut bills 70–90%). Latency optimizations are the last polish.
